incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only A (under-selecting, correct but incomplete)

params.description: Image A pulses to confirm selection.

steps.text: Money does use 10s. Look at the other images. Are there any others that group things by 10?

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only B (under-selecting, correct but incomplete)

params.description: Image B pulses to confirm selection.

steps.text: Measuring does use 10s. Look at the other images. Are there any others that group things by 10?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only C or nothing (all-wrong)

params.description: Images A and B dim slightly to draw attention.

steps.text: Scale of 10 means counting in groups of 10. Look at the money and the ruler. What pattern do you see on both?

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected C plus at least one correct answer (mixed correct and incorrect)

params.description: Images A and B highlight, C dims.

steps.text: You found situations that use 10s. But counting one by one doesn't use groups of 10. Which images show counting by 10s?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Images A and B highlight. Annotation appears: '10, 20, 30...' over both.

steps.text: Let me show you. Look at image A: dollar bills in stacks of 10. Look at image B: the ruler has marks at 10, 20, 30. Both count by 10s. Image C counts one by one, not in groups of 10. The answers are A and B. When you see bigger numbers organized in groups of 10, scale of 10 makes counting faster.
